features.name: Two-Mile (Chicago Avenue) Lake Tunnel
features.note: Chesbrough's original brick-lined lake tunnel, 5 ft dia., 10,567 ft (2 mi) from the Two-Mile/Chicago Avenue Crib to the Chicago Avenue Pumping Station (806 N Michigan Ave, still standing). Opened March 1867; a parallel 7-ft second tunnel added 1874 to a separate West Side (22nd St/Ashland Ave) pumping station. Straight-line approximation of the tunnel's true (also essentially straight) bore; crib and station coordinates per doc 03 (Wikipedia-sourced crib position) and OpenStreetMap (pumping station building). Closed and demolished 1936 when the crib was decommissioned.

features.name: 68th Street Lake Tunnel
features.note: Lake tunnel from the 68th Street Crib (2.3 mi east of 59th St Harbor) to the historic 68th Street Pumping Station on shore, commissioned 1892 for the South Side. The crib still stands but is inactive (doc 03); tunnel status/whether it still carries any flow is not documented in the archive, so 'to' is left null rather than guessed. Straight-line approximation; crib position from Wikipedia-sourced coordinates (doc 03), pumping station from OpenStreetMap.

features.name: Lake View Lake Tunnel
features.note: 2-mile (10,000 ft), 6-ft-diameter tunnel from the Lake View Crib to the Lake View Pumping Station -- the first Chicago water tunnel driven partly through rock (doc 03). Superseded in 1918 by a new, larger Clarendon Ave. tunnel serving the same pumping station directly; the crib's intake shaft was domed over in 1922 and the crib demolished in 1924 (HAER IL-4). Crib position is only approximate ('1.9 mi east of former Montrose Blvd' per doc 03, no coordinate found) -- built from that bearing description, not a surveyed point; pumping station from OpenStreetMap.

features.name: Carter H. Harrison Lake Tunnel
features.note: Lake tunnel from the Carter H. Harrison Crib (2.6 mi east of North Avenue Beach) to shore. Drained for inspection in 1997 over engineering objections and collapsed; the city spent $5.3M filling the tunnel under Lake Shore Drive near North Avenue in 1997-98, so the shore terminus here is placed at North Avenue Beach/LSD rather than at a pumping station (doc 03). The crib still stands, unused, since the 1998 collapse. Straight-line approximation; crib coordinates from Wikipedia (doc 03).

features.name: Wilson Avenue Crib Tunnel System (to Mayfair Pumping Station)
features.note: Approximate ~8-mile system of hand-dug rock tunnels from the Wilson Avenue Crib (2.1 mi east of Montrose Point, completed May 1, 1918 after an out-of-plumb superstructure had to be re-leveled with 300 jacks) inland to the Wilson Avenue/Mayfair pumping system (doc 03). No detailed tunnel-route map was located in this archive; the geometry here is a straight-line-through-waypoints sketch connecting the crib to the Mayfair Pumping Station (OpenStreetMap), not a traced alignment -- true tunnel routing underground is unlikely to run straight and is undocumented in sources reviewed.

features.name: William E. Dever Crib Tunnel (Chicago Avenue Tunnel)
features.note: 16-ft-diameter, 11.5-mile Chicago Avenue lake tunnel (130-200 ft deep, ~3,000,000 lb of dynamite used) connecting the William E. Dever Crib (built adjacent to the Harrison Crib, 1934-35) to the Chicago Avenue Pumping Station / Jardine Water Purification Plant area on the Near North Side lakefront (doc 03). Jardine (opened 1964/68) now receives this tunnel's flow; the historic Chicago Avenue Pumping Station building is immediately adjacent. Straight-line-through-waypoints approximation of an 11.5-mile bored tunnel whose true underground routing is not mapped in sources reviewed.

features.name: Edward F. Dunne Crib Tunnel (to Sawyer WPP)
features.note: Tunnel from the Edward F. Dunne Crib (built 1909, 50 ft from the 68th Street Crib, joined by footbridge) to shore, historically terminating at the 68th Street Pumping Station and now feeding the Eugene Sawyer Water Purification Plant (opened as the South Water Filtration Plant, 1947) at 79th Street (doc 03). Route drawn crib -> historic 68th St shore terminus -> modern Sawyer plant as a simplified proxy for the documented land legs; not a traced tunnel alignment.

features.name: Lawrence Avenue Conduit
features.note: 16-ft gravity conduit running from a Lake Michigan shore intake 'in the vicinity of Sheridan Road' near Wilson/Lawrence (per an 1901 Sanitary District Board of Engineers exhibit, doc 08) west under Lawrence Avenue, past the original inland Lawrence Avenue Sewage Pumping Station (1907-1930, at Lawrence Ave and the C.M.&St.P.R.R./Northwestern Elevated crossing east of Broadway, which lifted intercepted sewage ~10 ft into the conduit's head), to its outfall on the North Branch of the Chicago River at the site of today's North Branch Pumping Station (built 1928-1930 directly atop the outfall). Authorized 1898, substantially complete by 1907; also carried lake water pumped through via a 585-cfs screw pump to flush the North Branch. Still in active use today via NBPS. Lakefront intake and original inland pumping-station coordinates cross-checked against this project's companion facilities.json (points 'lawrence-conduit-intake' and 'mwrd-lawrence-original-ps'); route between the three points is a straight-line-through-waypoints sketch, not a traced conduit alignment.

features.name: 39th Street (Pershing Road) Conduit
features.note: 20-ft conduit authorized by the same 1898 City-Sanitary District ordinances as the Lawrence Avenue conduit (doc 08), running from a Lake Michigan shore intake near 39th Street west along the 39th St/Pershing Rd corridor to the South Fork of the South Branch near the Racine Avenue Pumping Station (doc 01/06/08, by analogy to the well-documented Lawrence Ave conduit's twin design). The lakefront terminus is placed near today's 41st Street Beach; the actual 1904 shoreline at 39th St sat considerably further west, before 20th-century Burnham Park landfill pushed the shore east -- flagged as a real limitation of this approximation, not resolved in sources reviewed. Racine Ave Pumping Station position from OpenStreetMap; the final short leg north to the pumping station along Racine Ave is a simplification of the true 1904 alignment, which is not documented in this archive.

features.name: TARP Mainstream Tunnel System
features.note: 40.5-mile, 1.2-billion-gallon-capacity deep tunnel (8-33 ft dia., 150-300 ft underground) running beneath the Wilmette Pumping Station / North Shore Channel, the North Branch and South Branch of the Chicago River, and the Chicago Sanitary & Ship Canal to the Mainstream Pumping Station at Hodgkins (doc 09). Built under contracts let 1975-1985 (first segment online 1985; full system complete 2006, $1.142B). Geometry approximates the tunnel's true alignment by reusing/downsampling the corresponding surface waterway corridor (OpenStreetMap), per the archive's own note that TARP tunnels 'FOLLOW the waterway corridors' -- the tunnel itself runs 150-300 ft below these surface features, not along them exactly.

features.name: TARP Des Plaines Tunnel System
features.note: 25.6-26.6-mile deep tunnel (10-33 ft dia.) along the Des Plaines River and its North Branch tributary, draining the northwest suburbs and converging with the Mainstream system near McCook/Hodgkins for pumping to Stickney WRP (doc 09). Built under contracts let across the Phase I era (1975-2006); MWRD's per-contract ledger does not give this specific system a single start year, so 'from:1999' here follows the task brief's approximation rather than a documented single date -- treat as a rough midpoint of a multi-decade build, not a firm start year. Geometry approximates the tunnel's alignment by downsampling the Des Plaines River's surface corridor (OpenStreetMap) between the northwest suburbs and the McCook/Hodgkins convergence; the tunnel runs 150-300 ft below this surface corridor, not along it exactly.

features.name: TARP Calumet Tunnel System
features.note: 36.7-mile deep tunnel (9-30 ft dia.) along the Calumet Sag Channel, the Little Calumet and Grand Calumet Rivers to near the Indiana state line, converging on the Calumet TARP Pumping Station (~365 ft below grade, custom 54x36-in eccentric reducing elbow) near the Calumet WRP in Riverdale, which lifts captured flow to the plant (doc 09). Built under Phase I contracts 1975-2006 (the Little Calumet leg, the last major segment, completed ~2002-2006); 'from:1986' follows the task brief's approximation of the system's main build window rather than a single documented start date. Geometry approximates the tunnel's alignment by downsampling the Little Calumet River and Cal-Sag Channel surface corridors (OpenStreetMap) and routing through the Calumet TARP Pumping Station point (cross-checked against this project's companion facilities.json point 'mwrd-calumet-tarp'); the tunnel runs well below these surface features, not along them exactly.

features.name: TARP Upper Des Plaines (O'Hare) Tunnel System
features.note: The smallest TARP Phase I system: 6.6 miles, 70 MG capacity, 9-20 ft dia., draining an 11-sq-mi combined-sewer area around O'Hare Airport (Des Plaines, Mount Prospect, Arlington Heights) toward the James C. Kirie WRP, via the Gloria Alitto Majewski Reservoir from 1998 onward (doc 09). Online 1981 -- the first TARP segment in service. Geometry is a straight-line placeholder between the general O'Hare area and the Majewski Reservoir (OpenStreetMap); no detailed alignment map for this short system was located in this archive.

features.name: North Side Intercepting Sewer Tunnel Crossing (under North Shore Channel at NBPS)
features.note: Small tunnel connecting the east-bank North Branch Pumping Station to the west-bank North Side Intercepting Sewer, dug under the North Shore Channel; MWRD photographed miners working in this tunnel on Oct. 26, 1929 (doc 08). Optional/minor feature -- exact dimensions and routing not documented in sources reviewed; geometry is a short straight-line placeholder from NBPS to a point just west across the channel.
